entries.title: Coder / Reviewer pattern
headings: What the review loop looks like
What teams get from the pattern
entries.body: Coder / Reviewer pattern Use this page when you need a practical starting point for understanding the Coder / Reviewer operating pattern inside You Agent Factory. Teams use this pattern when they want one role focused on producing the change and another role focused on validating whether that change should move forward. The split does not guarantee correctness, but it does make ownership clearer and makes review work explicit instead of optional. Who the two roles are Coder The coder is responsible for making the requested change real. In practice that usually means reading the task, inspecting the current system, editing code or docs, running the relevant checks, and showing enough evidence that the change is ready for review. The coder should optimize for correctness and traceability, not only speed. A strong coder handoff leaves the reviewer with concrete outputs to inspect: the diff, test results, and a short explanation of what changed. Reviewer The reviewer is responsible for deciding whether the change is acceptable to merge or whether it needs another pass. That means checking the actual behavior, reading the diff for design fit, verifying tests, and pushing back when the change does not meet the requested standard. The reviewer is not there to re-implement the task. The value of the role is independent judgment about the submitted work: does it solve the stated problem, does it preserve system quality, and is the evidence strong enough to approve? How the handoff works The normal handoff is simple: 1. The coder completes one bounded piece of work. 2. The coder runs the relevant checks and records what was verified. 3. The reviewer inspects the diff and the observable evidence. 4. The reviewer either approves the work or sends it back with concrete changes requested. This handoff keeps coding and judging separate. That separation matters because the person who made the change is often the least reliable person to decide that their own work is fully correct. What the review loop looks like In day-to-day engineering work, the loop is usually short and explicit: 1. The reviewer reads the change against the task, not only against personal style preferences. 2. If something is wrong or unproven, the reviewer sends specific feedback back to the coder. 3. The coder revises the change, reruns the relevant validation, and hands the update back. 4. The loop ends when the reviewer can either approve with confidence or reject because the requested outcome is still not being met. Good review loops are concrete. Feedback should point to the behavior that failed, the risk it creates, and what proof would resolve the concern. What teams get from the pattern Used well, the Coder / Reviewer pattern gives a team a few practical advantages: Better change quality because someone other than the author checks the result. Clearer review ownership because approval is a deliberate step rather than an implied one. More disciplined iteration because rejected work comes back with specific issues to fix. Those outcomes are useful because they improve merge decisions, not because the pattern removes uncertainty. The reviewer still works with incomplete information and has to apply engineering judgment. Where approval gates matter This pattern only works safely when approval is treated as a real gate. The reviewer should be able to stop the change from moving forward when the evidence is weak, the important behavior was not validated, or the implementation does not match the requested outcome. In practice, that usually means the reviewer does not approve until they can trace the request to the diff and to the checks that matter for that change. If the evidence is missing, the correct move is to reject the handoff and ask the coder to come back with stronger validation. Realistic limits and failure modes The most common failure mode is shallow review. A reviewer can read quickly, approve based on trust, and miss the exact behavior that needed scrutiny. Another failure mode is unclear ownership, where the coder assumes the reviewer will catch every issue and the reviewer assumes the coder already validated the risky parts. The reviewer role helps contain those risks by forcing one person to judge the submission independently, but it does not remove the need for engineering judgment. If the review is rushed, the task is ambiguous, or the evidence is weak, the pattern can still fail and should not be treated as automatic safety.

entries.title: Installation
entries.body: Installation Install You Agent Factory locally using the repository's supported Bun and Make workflow before moving into the first workflow run. Prerequisites Start with Bun 1.1 or newer available on your machine because the repository uses Bun for dependency installation, scripts, and test execution. Clone the repository and work from its root so the documented Make targets run against the same command path used by local contributors and CI. Install the repository Run make setup from the repository root to install or refresh dependencies. This target is the supported entrypoint for initial setup and it delegates to bun install instead of requiring a separate manual package-manager path. Validate the install Run make check, make test, and make build after setup to verify that the local install matches pull request validation. These are the authoritative repository-root commands for contributor and PR parity, so the same typecheck, lint, automated tests, and static export checks fail in both places without switching to a CI-only command surface. What success looks like Treat the install as successful when make check, make test, and make build finish without failures from the repository root. If you want the broader local-only foundation sweep before review, run make quality-gate afterward; it intentionally goes beyond PR validation by adding localization, content, accessibility, and static-export contract checks. Continue into quickstart Continue to Quickstart once the repository installs cleanly and the validation path passes so your first workflow run starts from a known-good local setup.

entries.title: Introduction
headings: What You Agent Factory helps you do
Who this setup path is for
entries.body: Introduction You Agent Factory is an open-source, engineering-native platform for turning recurring development work into reusable, inspectable AI agent workflows. What You Agent Factory helps you do Use it when your team has engineering tasks that happen over and over again, such as docs updates, review passes, release checks, or follow-up work after a bug fix, and you want those flows to be repeatable without becoming opaque. Instead of treating each run as a one-off chat, You Agent Factory helps you define a workflow, run it with agents and tools, inspect the outputs, and keep human review in the loop where approval still matters. Who this setup path is for This path is for engineers and technical teams who already work from repositories, files, command-line tools, and review processes, and want a clearer way to structure recurring AI-assisted development work. If you are evaluating whether the project fits your team, this lane gives you the smallest useful sequence: understand the product, install the supported local toolchain, and run one first outcome. Why continue into setup Continue if you want to confirm the local prerequisites and supported command path for this repository before you invest further time. The next pages show the Bun and Make workflow this repo already expects, then close the lane with one first workflow outcome before you move on to deeper guides and concepts. Continue through setup Move next to Installation, then Quickstart, to stay on the first-adoption path.

entries.title: Quickstart
entries.body: Quickstart Run the docs site locally after installation so the first setup path ends in one concrete, reviewer-verifiable outcome. Before you start Complete the Installation step first so the repository dependencies are installed and make check, make test, and make build already pass from the repository root. Start the local docs site Run bun run dev from the repository root. Use the local URL that Bun prints, then open the /docs route inside that running site so you stay in the docs shell instead of relying on homepage copy alone. Verify the first outcome Treat the quickstart as successful when the local docs shell renders the setup sequence and you can move through Introduction, Installation, and Quickstart from the generated sidebar or previous-next navigation. At that point you have confirmed the supported install path, the generated docs navigation, and the local rendering loop that future docs work builds on. Continue into deeper guides Continue into Getting started for the broader docs baseline, then move on to Core concepts when you want the orchestration model behind the site and factory.
